# 01 — Decouple snapshot write from TTL reset

Status: done
Labels: bug

*This was generated by AI during triage.*

## What

`SessionService.updateEncryptedState` (line 56) unconditionally sets `expiresAt = Instant.now().plus(7, ChronoUnit.DAYS)` on every snapshot write. This silently overrides any TTL the user set via the SessionPanel slider (e.g. 30 days becomes 7 days the first time anyone saves content).

## Root cause

```java
// SessionService.java:56
session.setExpiresAt(Instant.now().plus(7, ChronoUnit.DAYS));
```

This hardcodes 7 days regardless of the session's current `expiresAt`.

## Fix

Replace the hardcoded reset with a rolling extension that only advances `expiresAt` if the current expiry is less than 7 days away, leaving longer-lived sessions untouched:

```java
Instant sevenDaysFromNow = Instant.now().plus(7, ChronoUnit.DAYS);
if (session.getExpiresAt().isBefore(sevenDaysFromNow)) {
    session.setExpiresAt(sevenDaysFromNow);
}
```

This preserves user-set TTLs greater than 7 days while still extending short-lived or nearly-expired sessions on activity.

## Acceptance

- Set TTL to 30 days via the SessionPanel
- Write content (triggers a snapshot PUT)
- `GET /api/sessions/{id}` — `expiresAt` is still ~30 days from creation, not 7 days from now

## Files

- `src/main/java/com/parchie/service/SessionService.java` (line 56)
- `src/test/java/com/parchie/service/SessionServiceTest.java` — add a test case covering this invariant
